---
title: "Diblock Copolymer"
subtitle: "Your first pySEB model in Python and C++"
jupyter: python3
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  error: false
---

This tutorial joins two Gaussian polymers end to end, names the resulting
structure `diblock`, and evaluates its normalized form factor.

## Install the Python package

Create a dedicated environment. On Linux and macOS:

```bash
python3 -m venv .venv-pyseb
source .venv-pyseb/bin/activate
python -m pip install --upgrade pip
python -m pip install pyseb numpy matplotlib
```

On Windows PowerShell:

```powershell
py -3 -m venv .venv-pyseb
.venv-pyseb\Scripts\Activate.ps1
python -m pip install --upgrade pip
python -m pip install pyseb numpy matplotlib
```

Confirm the installation:

```bash
python -c "import pyseb; print(type(pyseb.World()).__name__)"
```

The command prints `World`. Keep the environment activated while running the
Python examples.

## Model

```text
blockA.end1 ---- blockA.end2 = blockB.end1 ---- blockB.end2
```

The link identifies `blockB.end1` with `blockA.end2`. The generated parameter
names contain the subunit names: `beta_blockA`, `Rg_blockA`, and so on.

## Python

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pyseb

world = pyseb.World("DiblockTutorial")
graph = world.Add("GaussianPolymer", "blockA")
world.Link("GaussianPolymer", "blockB.end1", "blockA.end2")
world.Add(graph, "diblock")

parameters = {
    "beta_blockA": 1.0,
    "beta_blockB": 0.8,
    "Rg_blockA": 5.0,
    "Rg_blockB": 8.0,
}
q = np.geomspace(1e-3, 0.5, 150)
form_factor = np.asarray(
    world.EvaluateFormFactor("diblock", parameters, q.tolist())
)

print(f"F({q[0]:g}) = {form_factor[0]:.8f}")
print(f"F({q[-1]:g}) = {form_factor[-1]:.8f}")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-python-diblock
#| fig-cap: "Normalized form factor of the Gaussian diblock."

plt.loglog(q, form_factor)
plt.xlabel(r"$q$")
plt.ylabel(r"$F(q)$")
plt.show()
```

The normalized result approaches one at small q and decreases as the polymer
length scales are resolved.

## C++

The maintained C++14 program performs the same construction and prints CSV.
Build pySEB using the [C++ platform guide](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Build-Guides),
or build the repository's `doc-tutorials` target.

```cpp
{{< include cpp/diblock.cpp >}}
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
from _helpers import run_tutorial

run_tutorial("diblock")
```

## Next steps

- [Attach polymers to a sphere](micelle.qmd)
- [Naming and linking reference](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Naming-and-Linking)
- [Gaussian polymer subunit](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Subunit-Gaussian-Polymer)
